method3.generated_article:
==Flora and fauna==
Howland Island’s terrestrial biota is sparse and shaped by its low, dry coral-island environment, with vegetation largely consisting of grasses, prostrate vines, and scattered low shrubs rather than forest.[1] The island’s plant cover has historically been limited by poor soils, salt exposure, and scarce freshwater, producing a simple flora typical of central Pacific atolls and uplifted reef islands.[1]

Despite its limited terrestrial vegetation, Howland Island is an important seabird habitat, supporting large nesting and roosting colonies of tropical oceanic birds.[1] Species recorded from the island have included boobies, frigatebirds, terns, noddies, and tropicbirds, reflecting the island’s significance as an undisturbed breeding site in the equatorial Pacific.[1] The abundance of nesting seabirds has also historically contributed nutrients to the island ecosystem through guano deposition.[1]

Land fauna is otherwise very limited, with no native terrestrial mammals and only a small number of invertebrates and reptiles able to persist in the harsh environment.[1] Marine life in the surrounding waters is considerably richer than the island’s land biota, with coral-reef and pelagic species forming the broader ecological setting of Howland Island National Wildlife Refuge.[1]

==Economics==
Howland Island has no permanent inhabitants and no settled local economy.[1]  
Historically, the island’s principal economic use was the extraction of guano after the United States claimed it under the Guano Act of 1856, but those deposits are now exhausted.[1]  
In the 1930s, the atoll also had a limited strategic and transportation role as a stop for aircraft flying between Hawaii and Australia.[1]  
In the present day, economic activity is minimal, as the island is administered as a U.S. National Wildlife Refuge and forms part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[1]  
Howland Island is also part of the Pacific Remote Island Areas and associated U.S. Exclusive Economic Zone, though the documents provided do not indicate any island-based commercial development or resident workforce.[3][5]

==Time zone==
Howland Island observes UTC−12:00, making it one of the westernmost time zones in civil use.[1][2] This offset is shared with nearby Baker Island and is commonly identified with the International Date Line West or IDLW zone.[1][3] Because Howland Island is uninhabited, UTC−12:00 is often noted in discussions of the “Anywhere on Earth” convention, under which a calendar day is considered to end when it ends on Howland and Baker Islands.[3][4] The island’s position in UTC−12 also places it 26 hours behind the Line Islands of Kiribati, which use UTC+14:00 after Kiribati shifted the date line in 1995 to place the whole country on the same calendar day.[1][5] Although the day ends later on Howland Island than in any inhabited place, the last inhabited territories to observe a given date are generally American Samoa and Niue at UTC−11:00.[3][5]

==History==
Several American whaling vessels sighted and charted the island in the early 19th century, with the first recorded sighting commonly dated to 1822.[2][4] An alternative tradition attributes its discovery to the American mariner Edmund Fanning in 1791, but later accounts identify the 1822 sighting as the first recorded one.[1][2] In 1842, the island was renamed Howland Island after a lookout from an American whaling ship, though one account specifically credits Captain George E. Netcher of the whaleship *Isabella* with applying the name.[2][4]

The United States claimed Howland Island in 1857 under the Guano Islands Act of 1856.[2][4] During the 19th century, both American and British interests mined the island’s guano deposits, and a legal dispute between competing U.S. companies in 1865 ended with both being allowed to work the deposits.[3][4] Commercial guano extraction ceased after the deposits were exhausted, with sources giving the end of mining as 1878 or, more broadly, before 1890.[3][4]

In 1935, Howland was colonized from Hawaii as part of the American Equatorial Islands Colonization Project, also known as the Hui Panalaʻau Project, to establish a permanent U.S. presence in the central Pacific.[2][4] A small settlement called Itascatown was established on the island and administered under the U.S. Department of the Interior from 1936.[2][4] During this period, Howland was developed as a potential refueling and stopover point for trans-Pacific aviation between Hawaii and Australia.[2]

Howland Island became widely known in connection with Amelia Earhart’s attempted circumnavigational flight in 1937.[1][2] On July 2, 1937, Earhart and her navigator, Frederick J. Noonan, were lost near Howland after departing Lae, Papua New Guinea, while attempting to reach the island.[2]

During World War II, the island was used by the United States military as a lookout post.[1] In the postwar period, it remained without permanent inhabitants and was managed primarily for conservation.[2] It is now protected as a National Wildlife Refuge and, since 2009, has formed part of the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument.[2]

==National Wildlife Refuge==
Howland Island is administered as part of the U.S. National Wildlife Refuge System, with refuge status established in 1974.[4] The refuge originally included the island, its waters, and submerged and emergent lands from the mean low-water line out to 3 nautical miles.[4] In 2009, its protection was expanded when the island was included in the Pacific Remote Islands Marine National Monument, and the refuge boundary was extended from 3 to 12 nautical miles.[3][4] The wider monument area from 0 to 50 nautical miles around Howland is managed cooperatively by the U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service and the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration.[4]

The refuge protects an uninhabited coral island and surrounding reef ecosystem that provide nesting and foraging habitat for seabirds and other marine wildlife.[1][3] The island is ringed by an active fringing reef, and high-resolution imagery has been used to improve mapping and environmental assessment of its reefs and nearby seafloor.[1] The surrounding protected area is also important for threatened and endangered species, including sea turtles, and the broader monument has been noted for especially high densities of green sea turtles in the U.S. Pacific.[3][4]

Human access is highly restricted, and there are no public uses proposed for the refuge.[5] Visits are generally limited to scientists, researchers, and U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service personnel conducting monitoring and management work.[2] Refuge planning documents have emphasized continued biological surveys, habitat assessment, seabird-restoration efforts, and expanded marine research, including the possibility of using the islands as baseline sites for climate-change research.[5] Environmental protections prohibit extractive and other harmful activities in order to conserve the island’s terrestrial and marine ecosystems.[2]

==Earhart Light==
A small navigational light, later known as Earhart Light, was erected on Howland Island in 1937 in connection with preparations for Amelia Earhart's intended refueling stop there during her round-the-world flight.[1][3] Contemporary accounts describe it as a lighthouse or light beacon built that year, alongside improvements to the island's landing area for Earhart's expected arrival.[3][5]

Earhart never reached Howland Island, and the beacon instead became a memorial associated with her disappearance.[1][3] A plaque honoring Earhart was mounted on the structure, but it was later damaged during wartime attacks.[5]

The light had only a brief operational life.[3][5] During the Japanese attack on Howland Island in December 1941, bombing and shelling damaged the settlement and rendered the Amelia Earhart light beacon unusable.[2] Other accounts state that the lighthouse was partially destroyed in the attack and became inactive by 1942.[4][5]

After the war, the structure remained abandoned, and the former light station was thereafter regarded only as a day beacon rather than an active lighthouse.[3][5] It was rebuilt or restored in the early 1960s, and later visits also added the names of the two colonists killed in the 1941 attack.[2][4]

In its present form, Earhart Light survives as a ruined or abandoned landmark on the otherwise uninhabited island.[3][4] Its historical significance lies less in navigational use than in its association with Earhart's final flight and with the brief prewar settlement of Howland Island.[1][2]

==Image gallery==
A frequently reproduced modern image of Howland Island is an astronaut photograph taken from the International Space Station on 3 December 2004 during Expedition 10.[2][3] The image depicts the island as a small coral-fragment landmass encircled by white breakers that mark its active fringing reef.[2][4] NASA has noted that such high-resolution photographs are used to update maps of reefs and islands, assess reef ecosystem health, and estimate surrounding bathymetry.[2][4] The photograph was made with a Kodak DCS760C electronic still camera fitted with an 800 mm lens from an altitude of about 192 nautical miles (356 km).[2][3]

Gallery and educational materials about Howland Island also commonly include a simple locator or map image showing the island’s position in the central Pacific.[1] Such visual material typically emphasizes the island’s remoteness, small size, and present importance as protected habitat for seabirds and other marine wildlife rather than as a settled landscape.[1][2][5]
